Optimal Transport for Machine Learners

Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceStatistical Optimal TransportGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation

Semi-discrete and Wasserstein-1

This chapter focuses on two computationally useful degeneracies of the dual problem. Semi-discrete optimal transport turns a continuous-to-discrete map into finite-dimensional geometry, while W1\Wass_1W1​ replaces convex potentials by Lipschitz functions and flow fields. The material connects computational geometry Aurenhammer et al., 1998Mérigot, 2011Mérigot, 2013 with the Kantorovich--Rubinstein and Beckmann formulations Kantorovich & Rubinstein, 1958Beckmann, 1952.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Semi-dual¶

The semi-dual eliminates one potential by an exact ccc-transform. It keeps concavity while removing explicit inequality constraints.

Write the dual problem as

sup⁡f,g∈C(X)×C(Y)E(f,g),\sup_{f,g\in\Cc(\X)\times\Cc(\Y)} \mathcal{E}(f,g),f,g∈C(X)×C(Y)sup​E(f,g),
(1)

where E(f,g)\mathcal{E}(f,g)E(f,g) is the dual objective, with value −∞-\infty−∞ when the feasibility constraint fails. Optimizing out ggg exactly gives the semi-dual problem

sup⁡f∈C(X)E~(f):=E(f,fc)=sup⁡gE(f,g)=∫Xf  dα+∫Yfc  dβ.\sup_{f\in\Cc(\X)} \widetilde{\mathcal{E}}(f) \eqdef \mathcal{E}(f,f^c) = \sup_g \mathcal{E}(f,g) = \int_\X f\,\d\alpha + \int_\Y f^c\,\d\beta .f∈C(X)sup​E(f):=E(f,fc)=gsup​E(f,g)=∫X​fdα+∫Y​fcdβ.
(2)

Partial maximization of a concave problem preserves concavity, so E~\widetilde{\mathcal{E}}E is still concave. The advantage is that the explicit inequality constraint has disappeared, which allows simpler optimization algorithms.

Semi-discrete¶

The semi-discrete case is the setting where dual potentials become weights of Laguerre cells. This gives both geometry and algorithms for quantization and density fitting.

Discrete Targets and Laguerre Cells¶

Consider the case where

β=∑j=1mbjδyj\beta=\sum_{j=1}^m b_j\delta_{y_j}β=j=1∑m​bj​δyj​​
(3)

is discrete. The same construction applies if α\alphaα is discrete, after exchanging the roles of α\alphaα and β\betaβ. Restricting the minimization in Definition Definition: ccc-Transform to the support of β\betaβ, equivalently applying that definition with the discrete target space Y={yj}j=1m\Y=\{y_j\}_{j=1}^mY={yj​}j=1m​ and identifying a vector g∈Rmg\in\RR^mg∈Rm with the function g:Y→Rg:\Y\to\RRg:Y→R defined by g(yj)=gjg(y_j)=g_jg(yj​)=gj​, gives the discrete cˉ\bar ccˉ-transform

gcˉ(x):=min⁡1≤j≤mc(x,yj)−gj.g^{\bar c}(x) \eqdef \min_{1\le j\le m} c(x,y_j)-g_j.gcˉ(x):=1≤j≤mmin​c(x,yj​)−gj​.
(4)

This maps a vector ggg to a continuous function under the same regularity assumptions on ccc as in the continuous setting. Using this transform when β\betaβ is discrete yields the finite-dimensional semi-dual

Lc(α,β)=max⁡g∈RmE(g):=∫Xgcˉ(x)  dα(x)+∑j=1mgjbj.\mathcal{L}_c(\alpha,\beta) = \max_{g\in\RR^m} \mathcal{E}(g) \eqdef \int_\X g^{\bar c}(x)\,\d\alpha(x) + \sum_{j=1}^m g_j b_j .Lc​(α,β)=g∈Rmmax​E(g):=∫X​gcˉ(x)dα(x)+j=1∑m​gj​bj​.
(5)

The geometric object encoded by the dual weights is a weighted nearest-neighbor diagram: each source point is assigned to the target atom that realizes the discrete cˉ\bar ccˉ-transform.

Definition: Laguerre Cells and Power Diagrams

For sites (yj)j=1m(y_j)_{j=1}^m(yj​)j=1m​ and weights g∈Rmg\in\RR^mg∈Rm, the Laguerre cell associated with yjy_jyj​ is

Lj(g):={x∈X:c(x,yj)−gj≤c(x,yj′)−gj′for all j′≠j}.\mathcal{L}_j(g) \eqdef \left\{ x\in\X : c(x,y_j)-g_j \le c(x,y_{j'})-g_{j'} \quad\text{for all }j'\ne j \right\}.Lj​(g):={x∈X:c(x,yj​)−gj​≤c(x,yj′​)−gj′​for all j′=j}.
(6)

The cells cover X\XX; after arbitrary tie-breaking on common boundaries, they induce a disjoint partition. When c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2, this decomposition is also called a power diagram. If ggg is constant, it reduces to the ordinary Voronoi diagram.

For quadratic costs, varying the dual weights moves the walls between adjacent cells while keeping them parallel. This is the geometric mechanism by which the cell masses are adjusted.

show_book_figure("semidiscrete-laguerre-cells")

Laguerre cells for semi-discrete quadratic transport. The red contours show a continuous source density α\alphaα given by a three-component Gaussian mixture on the right. The twenty-one colored circular sites are the atoms of the discrete target β\betaβ, sampled from a compact cloud on the left; each site color matches its Laguerre cell. Starting from ordinary Voronoi cells, semi-dual weight updates deform the cells so that the α\alphaα-mass captured by each cell approaches the prescribed target mass.

The interactive demo exposes the dual-weight mechanism directly. Increase the number of weight updates to watch cells with too little mass expand and cells with too much mass shrink.

Interactive panel. Use the weight and seed controls to deform Laguerre cells and watch how their areas respond to semi-discrete masses.

Mass Balance¶

The semi-dual energy can be rewritten as

E(g)=∑j=1m∫Lj(g)(c(x,yj)−gj)  dα(x)+⟨g,b⟩.\mathcal{E}(g) = \sum_{j=1}^m \int_{\mathcal{L}_j(g)} \left(c(x,y_j)-g_j\right)\,\d\alpha(x) + \langle g,b\rangle .E(g)=j=1∑m​∫Lj​(g)​(c(x,yj​)−gj​)dα(x)+⟨g,b⟩.
(7)
Proposition: Gradient of the Semi-discrete Dual

If α\alphaα gives zero mass to the Laguerre cell boundaries, then E\mathcal{E}E is differentiable at ggg and

∂E∂gj(g)=bj−∫Lj(g) dα.\frac{\partial \mathcal{E}}{\partial g_j}(g) = b_j-\int_{\mathcal{L}_j(g)}\d\alpha .∂gj​∂E​(g)=bj​−∫Lj​(g)​dα.
(8)
Proof

For α\alphaα-almost every xxx, the minimizing index in min⁡jc(x,yj)−gj\min_j c(x,y_j)-g_jminj​c(x,yj​)−gj​ is unique. If this index is j(x)j(x)j(x), then the directional derivative in direction h∈Rmh\in\RR^mh∈Rm is

 d dt∣t=0min⁡j(c(x,yj)−gj−thj)=−hj(x).\left.\frac{\d}{\d t}\right|_{t=0} \min_j\left(c(x,y_j)-g_j-t h_j\right) = -h_{j(x)}.dtd​∣∣​t=0​jmin​(c(x,yj​)−gj​−thj​)=−hj(x)​.
(9)

Dominated convergence gives

 dE(g)[h]=−∑jhj∫Lj(g) dα+∑jhjbj,\d\mathcal{E}(g)[h] = -\sum_j h_j\int_{\mathcal{L}_j(g)}\d\alpha + \sum_j h_j b_j,dE(g)[h]=−j∑​hj​∫Lj​(g)​dα+j∑​hj​bj​,
(10)

which is the announced gradient formula.

The first-order optimality condition says that solving the semi-discrete dual amounts to choosing weights ggg so that

∫Lj(g) dα=bjfor every j.\int_{\mathcal{L}_j(g)}\d\alpha=b_j \qquad\text{for every }j.∫Lj​(g)​dα=bj​for every j.
(11)

At optimality, the transport map is piecewise constant: it sends x∈Lj(g)x\in\mathcal{L}_j(g)x∈Lj​(g) to yjy_jyj​. For the quadratic cost, uniqueness follows from Brenier’s theorem when α\alphaα has a density.

The sign of the gradient has a direct geometric interpretation. Increasing gjg_jgj​ lowers the corresponding power distance and expands Lj(g)\mathcal L_j(g)Lj​(g); decreasing gjg_jgj​ shrinks it. The dotted outline marks the balanced cell, so semi-dual ascent can be read as a mass-balancing procedure on a power diagram.

show_book_figure("semidiscrete-weight-gradient-cells", width=760)

Dual weights control Laguerre cell masses in the semi-discrete quadratic problem. The same blue target sites and red Gaussian source density are used in all panels; only the highlighted violet weight is changed. The dotted violet outline is the balanced cell. If the highlighted cell has too little source mass, then bj−α(Lj(g))>0b_j-\alpha(\mathcal L_j(g))>0bj​−α(Lj​(g))>0 and the ascent update increases the weight, expanding the cell outward. If it has too much mass, the update decreases the weight, shrinking it inward. At balance, the cell mass matches the prescribed target mass and the first-order update vanishes.

Interactive panel. Vary the target weights and the number of dual updates to watch Laguerre cells rebalance their masses.

Quadratic power diagrams have polyhedral cells and can be computed efficiently using computational-geometry algorithms Aurenhammer, 1987Aurenhammer et al., 1998Mérigot, 2011. One classical construction lifts sites to (yj,∥yj∥2−gj)∈Rd+1(y_j,\norm{y_j}^2-g_j)\in\RR^{d+1}(yj​,∥yj​∥2−gj​)∈Rd+1 and obtains the power diagram by projecting the lower envelope of their convex hull. In dimensions two and three, Chan’s output-sensitive convex-hull algorithm has complexity O(mlog⁡Q)O(m\log Q)O(mlogQ) for mmm sites and QQQ hull vertices Chan, 1996.

Stochastic Optimization¶

The semi-discrete formulation is useful because the objective is an expectation with respect to α\alphaα:

E(g)=∫XE(g,x)  dα(x)=EX(E(g,X)),E(g,x):=gcˉ(x)+⟨g,b⟩.\mathcal{E}(g) = \int_\X E(g,x)\,\d\alpha(x) = \EE_X(E(g,X)), \qquad E(g,x)\eqdef g^{\bar c}(x)+\langle g,b\rangle .E(g)=∫X​E(g,x)dα(x)=EX​(E(g,X)),E(g,x):=gcˉ(x)+⟨g,b⟩.
(12)

Away from cell boundaries, the stochastic gradient of the integrand is

∇gE(g,x)=(bj−1Lj(g)(x))j=1m,\nabla_g E(g,x) = \left(b_j-\mathbf{1}_{\mathcal{L}_j(g)}(x)\right)_{j=1}^m,∇g​E(g,x)=(bj​−1Lj​(g)​(x))j=1m​,
(13)

an unbiased estimator of ∇E(g)\nabla\mathcal{E}(g)∇E(g) when cell boundaries have α\alphaα-measure zero. One can therefore maximize the semi-dual without first discretizing α\alphaα: the measure is used as a black box from which independent samples are drawn, a natural setup in high-dimensional statistics and machine learning.

Starting from g(0)=0g^{(0)}=0g(0)=0, stochastic gradient ascent draws xℓ∼αx_\ell\sim\alphaxℓ​∼α and performs

g(ℓ+1):=g(ℓ)+τℓ∇gE(g(ℓ),xℓ).g^{(\ell+1)} \eqdef g^{(\ell)} + \tau_\ell\nabla_g E(g^{(\ell)},x_\ell).g(ℓ+1):=g(ℓ)+τℓ​∇g​E(g(ℓ),xℓ​).
(14)

The step size must decay so that sampling noise averages out. A typical schedule is

τℓ:=τ01+ℓ/ℓ0.\tau_\ell\eqdef\frac{\tau_0}{1+\ell/\ell_0}.τℓ​:=1+ℓ/ℓ0​τ0​​.
(15)

Under standard stochastic-approximation assumptions,

E(g⋆)−E(E(g(ℓ)))=O(ℓ−1/2),\mathcal{E}(g^\star) - \EE\left(\mathcal{E}(g^{(\ell)})\right) = O(\ell^{-1/2}),E(g⋆)−E(E(g(ℓ)))=O(ℓ−1/2),
(16)

where g⋆g^\starg⋆ is a maximizer and the expectation is over the i.i.d. samples. This stochastic viewpoint is one of the main algorithmic advantages of the semi-discrete formulation Mérigot, 2011Genevay et al., 2016.

Algorithm: Semi-discrete Laguerre descent

Input: Source measure α\alphaα, target atoms (yj,bj)(y_j,\b_j)(yj​,bj​), cost ccc, steps τk\tau_kτk​.

Output: Semi-discrete dual weights g\gDg and Laguerre cells.

Initialize: Set g(0)=0\gD^{(0)}=0g(0)=0.

For k=0,1,…k=0,1,\ldotsk=0,1,… do:

Compute cells: Lj(g(k))={x  :  c(x,yj)−gj(k)≤c(x,yℓ)−gℓ(k)∀ℓ}.\Laguerre_j(\gD^{(k)}) = \enscond{x}{c(x,y_j)-\gD^{(k)}_j\leq c(x,y_\ell)-\gD^{(k)}_\ell\quad\forall \ell}.Lj​(g(k))={x:c(x,yj​)−gj(k)​≤c(x,yℓ​)−gℓ(k)​∀ℓ}.

Compute masses: mj(k)=∫Lj(g(k)) dα.m_j^{(k)}=\int_{\Laguerre_j(\gD^{(k)})}\d\al .mj(k)​=∫Lj​(g(k))​dα.

Update g(k+1)=g(k)+τk(b−m(k)).\gD^{(k+1)} = \gD^{(k)}+\tau_k\bigl(\b-m^{(k)}\bigr).g(k+1)=g(k)+τk​(b−m(k)).

If max⁡j∣mj(k)−bj∣≤tol\max_j\abs{m_j^{(k)}-\b_j}\leq\mathrm{tol}maxj​∣∣​mj(k)​−bj​∣∣​≤tol then:

Return g(k+1)\gD^{(k+1)}g(k+1) and the cells.

Algorithm: Stochastic semi-discrete ascent

Input: Source sampler x∼αx\sim\alphax∼α, target atoms (yj,bj)(y_j,\b_j)(yj​,bj​), steps τℓ\tau_\ellτℓ​.

Output: Stochastic semi-discrete dual weights g\gDg.

Initialize: Set g(0)=0\gD^{(0)}=0g(0)=0.

For ℓ=0,1,…\ell=0,1,\ldotsℓ=0,1,… do:

Draw xℓ∼αx_\ell\sim\alphaxℓ​∼α.

Set jℓ=min⁡arg min⁡j(c(xℓ,yj)−gj(ℓ))j_\ell=\min\argmin_j\bigl(c(x_\ell,y_j)-\gD_j^{(\ell)}\bigr)jℓ​=minargminj​(c(xℓ​,yj​)−gj(ℓ)​).

For j=1,…,mj=1,\ldots,mj=1,…,m do

gj(ℓ+1)=gj(ℓ)+τℓ(bj−1{j=jℓ}).\gD_j^{(\ell+1)} = \gD_j^{(\ell)} + \tau_\ell\bigl(\b_j-\ones_{\{j=j_\ell\}}\bigr).gj(ℓ+1)​=gj(ℓ)​+τℓ​(bj​−1{j=jℓ​}​).

Return g(ℓ)\gD^{(\ell)}g(ℓ) or its running average.

Optimal Quantization¶

Optimal quantization asks for the best discrete approximation of a measure by mmm codepoints. It is the geometric core of vector quantization, compression and kkk-means clustering.

For a measure α\alphaα, define

Qm(α):=min⁡Y=(yj)j=1m,  b∈ΔmWp(α,∑j=1mbjδyj).\mathcal{Q}_m(\alpha) \eqdef \min_{Y=(y_j)_{j=1}^m,\;b\in\simplex_m} \Wass_p\left(\alpha,\sum_{j=1}^m b_j\delta_{y_j}\right).Qm​(α):=Y=(yj​)j=1m​,b∈Δm​min​Wp​(α,j=1∑m​bj​δyj​​).
(17)

This problem is classical in approximation theory and information theory Graf & Luschgy, 2000Lloyd, 1982.

Free Masses and Prescribed Weights¶

The formulation (17) is the free-mass version of quantization: one optimizes both the codepoint positions Y=(yj)jY=(y_j)_jY=(yj​)j​ and the weights b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​. Prescribing the weights gives a different family of problems. The equal-weight case, bj=1/mb_j=1/mbj​=1/m, is especially important for particle approximations and is isolated at the end of this section.

Proposition: Quantization Rate and Curse of Dimensionality

Let Ω⊂Rd\Omega\subset\RR^dΩ⊂Rd be a bounded Lipschitz domain and assume α=ρ  dx\alpha=\rho\,\d xα=ρdx on Ω\OmegaΩ, with 0<ρ−≤ρ≤ρ+<+∞0<\rho_-\le\rho\le\rho_+<+\infty0<ρ−​≤ρ≤ρ+​<+∞. Then, for fixed p≥1p\ge1p≥1, there exist constants 0<c≤C<+∞0<c\le C<+\infty0<c≤C<+∞ such that

c m−1/d≤Qm(α)≤C m−1/d.c\,m^{-1/d} \le \mathcal{Q}_m(\alpha) \le C\,m^{-1/d}.cm−1/d≤Qm​(α)≤Cm−1/d.
(18)
Proof

For the upper bound, partition Ω\OmegaΩ into mmm cells of diameter at most Cm−1/dC m^{-1/d}Cm−1/d, up to boundary effects, and place one codepoint in each nonempty cell. Sending each point to the codepoint in its cell gives a transport distance bounded by Cm−1/dC m^{-1/d}Cm−1/d.

For the lower bound, fix any set YYY of mmm codepoints and write dY(x)=min⁡j∥x−yj∥d_Y(x)=\min_j\norm{x-y_j}dY​(x)=minj​∥x−yj​∥. Since the density is bounded above, the mass of the ttt-neighborhood of YYY is at most CmtdCmt^dCmtd. Choosing t0≃m−1/dt_0\simeq m^{-1/d}t0​≃m−1/d small enough gives α({dY>t})≥c\alpha(\{d_Y>t\})\ge cα({dY​>t})≥c for 0<t<t00<t<t_00<t<t0​. Hence

∫dY(x)p  dα(x)=∫0+∞ptp−1α({dY>t})  dt≥ct0p≃cm−p/d.\int d_Y(x)^p\,\d\alpha(x) = \int_0^{+\infty} p t^{p-1}\alpha(\{d_Y>t\})\,\d t \ge c t_0^p \simeq c m^{-p/d}.∫dY​(x)pdα(x)=∫0+∞​ptp−1α({dY​>t})dt≥ct0p​≃cm−p/d.
(19)

Taking the ppp-th root and minimizing over YYY proves the lower bound.

This deterministic rate mirrors the empirical optimal-transport sample-complexity rate: both are governed by the spacing m−1/dm^{-1/d}m−1/d of points in dimension ddd. Quantization is best-case and deterministic, while empirical OT is random, but both display the same curse of dimensionality.

For fixed codepoints YYY, the problem is convex with respect to the weights bbb. The dependence on YYY is nonconvex and is generally computationally hard. The rest of this section distinguishes the free-mass Lloyd reduction from the fixed-weight geometry underlying finite-particle W2\mathcal W_2W2​ gradient flows.

Lloyd Algorithm¶

The computational appeal of quantization comes from splitting the nonconvex search over sites into two elementary operations. For fixed sites, the optimal assignment is purely local: each point is sent to one of its nearest sites, and the resulting cells are Voronoi cells. This is the assignment step behind Lloyd’s algorithm and the kkk-means method.

Proposition: Free Masses Give Voronoi Cells

For the cost c(x,y)=d(x,y)pc(x,y)=d(x,y)^pc(x,y)=d(x,y)p, fix distinct codepoints Y=(yj)j=1mY=(y_j)_{j=1}^mY=(yj​)j=1m​. Duplicate codepoints can be merged beforehand. Minimizing over the weights b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​ gives

min⁡b∈ΔmWpp(α,∑jbjδyj)=∫Xmin⁡1≤j≤mc(x,yj)  dα(x).\min_{b\in\simplex_m} \Wass_p^p \left(\alpha,\sum_j b_j\delta_{y_j}\right) = \int_\X \min_{1\le j\le m} c(x,y_j)\,\d\alpha(x).b∈Δm​min​Wpp​(α,j∑​bj​δyj​​)=∫X​1≤j≤mmin​c(x,yj​)dα(x).
(20)

An optimal coupling is induced by sending each xxx to a nearest codepoint. The corresponding cells are the Voronoi cells

Vj(Y):={x:c(x,yj)≤c(x,yj′)for all j′},\mathcal{V}_j(Y) \eqdef \left\{ x : c(x,y_j)\le c(x,y_{j'}) \quad\text{for all }j' \right\},Vj​(Y):={x:c(x,yj​)≤c(x,yj′​)for all j′},
(21)

up to arbitrary tie-breaking on common boundaries.

Proof

For any coupling between α\alphaα and a measure supported on YYY, the conditional destination of a point xxx belongs to YYY, so its conditional cost is at least min⁡jc(x,yj)\min_j c(x,y_j)minj​c(x,yj​). Integrating gives the lower bound. Conversely, choose a measurable nearest-codepoint map TY(x)∈arg min⁡jc(x,yj)T_Y(x)\in\operatorname*{arg\,min}_j c(x,y_j)TY​(x)∈argminj​c(x,yj​), breaking ties measurably, and set bj=α(TY−1(yj))b_j=\alpha(T_Y^{-1}(y_j))bj​=α(TY−1​(yj​)). Then (TY)♯α=∑jbjδyj(T_Y)_\sharp\alpha=\sum_j b_j\delta_{y_j}(TY​)♯​α=∑j​bj​δyj​​ and the induced transport reaches the displayed lower bound.

Consequently, the quantization energy can be written in nearest-centroid form:

Qm(α)p=min⁡YF(Y),F(Y):=∫Xmin⁡1≤j≤mc(x,yj)  dα(x).\mathcal{Q}_m(\alpha)^p = \min_Y \mathcal{F}(Y), \qquad \mathcal{F}(Y) \eqdef \int_\X \min_{1\le j\le m} c(x,y_j)\,\d\alpha(x).Qm​(α)p=Ymin​F(Y),F(Y):=∫X​1≤j≤mmin​c(x,yj​)dα(x).
(22)

At a differentiability point of this energy, each local minimizer satisfies the centroid condition

yj∈arg min⁡y∫Vj(Y)c(x,y)  dα(x).y_j \in \operatorname*{arg\,min}_{y} \int_{\mathcal{V}_j(Y)} c(x,y)\,\d\alpha(x).yj​∈yargmin​∫Vj​(Y)​c(x,y)dα(x).
(23)

For the squared Euclidean cost, this becomes

yj=∫Vj(Y)x  dα(x)∫Vj(Y) dα.y_j = \frac{\int_{\mathcal{V}_j(Y)} x\,\d\alpha(x)} {\int_{\mathcal{V}_j(Y)} \d\alpha}.yj​=∫Vj​(Y)​dα∫Vj​(Y)​xdα(x)​.
(24)

Lloyd’s algorithm, also known as the kkk-means algorithm, iterates this fixed point: assign points to nearest sites, then replace each site by the centroid of its cell Lloyd, 1982. With standard tie-breaking, the objective decreases at each step. Since the problem is nonconvex in YYY, the iterates generally converge only to a local minimum. Good seeding matters; for squared Euclidean costs, kkk-means++ gives a logarithmic approximation guarantee in expectation Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007.

Continuous Lloyd Flow¶

There is also an infinitesimal version of Lloyd’s fixed point, but it should first be understood on finite labelled configurations. Assume that c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2 and that α\alphaα does not charge Voronoi boundaries. For a configuration YYY, define, on nonempty cells,

aj(Y)=α(Vj(Y)),bj(Y)=1aj(Y)∫Vj(Y)x  dα(x)a_j(Y)=\alpha(\mathcal V_j(Y)), \qquad b_j(Y)=\frac{1}{a_j(Y)} \int_{\mathcal V_j(Y)}x\,\d\alpha(x)aj​(Y)=α(Vj​(Y)),bj​(Y)=aj​(Y)1​∫Vj​(Y)​xdα(x)
(25)

as the cell mass and centroid. Empty cells are singular points of the vector field; one either freezes them, as in the algorithm below, or reseeds them. The relaxed step

yj(k+1)=yj(k)+τ(bj(Y(k))−yj(k)),0<τ≤1,y_j^{(k+1)} = y_j^{(k)}+\tau\big(b_j(Y^{(k)})-y_j^{(k)}\big), \qquad 0<\tau\le 1,yj(k+1)​=yj(k)​+τ(bj​(Y(k))−yj(k)​),0<τ≤1,
(26)

formally converges, as τ→0\tau\to0τ→0, to the cell-mass preconditioned gradient flow of the quantization energy F\mathcal FF,

y˙j(t)=bj(Yt)−yj(t).\dot y_j(t)=b_j(Y_t)-y_j(t).y˙​j​(t)=bj​(Yt​)−yj​(t).
(27)

Indeed, at differentiability points of F\mathcal FF, the envelope theorem gives

∇yjF(Y)=2∫Vj(Y)(yj−x) dα(x)=2aj(Y)(yj−bj(Y)),\nabla_{y_j}\mathcal F(Y) = 2\int_{\mathcal V_j(Y)}(y_j-x)\d\alpha(x) = 2a_j(Y)(y_j-b_j(Y)),∇yj​​F(Y)=2∫Vj​(Y)​(yj​−x)dα(x)=2aj​(Y)(yj​−bj​(Y)),
(28)

so that

y˙j(t)=−12aj(Yt)∇yjF(Yt).\dot y_j(t) = -\frac{1}{2a_j(Y_t)}\nabla_{y_j}\mathcal F(Y_t).y˙​j​(t)=−2aj​(Yt​)1​∇yj​​F(Yt​).
(29)

Equivalently, this is the gradient flow of F\mathcal FF for the site metric gY(U,V)=2∑jaj(Y)⟨uj,vj⟩g_Y(U,V)=2\sum_j a_j(Y)\langle u_j,v_j\ranglegY​(U,V)=2∑j​aj​(Y)⟨uj​,vj​⟩; it is not the unweighted Euclidean gradient flow unless the masses are absorbed into the time step. Along smooth portions of the flow,

 d dtF(Yt)=−2∑jaj(Yt)∥bj(Yt)−yj(t)∥2≤0.\frac{\d}{\d t}\mathcal F(Y_t) = -2\sum_j a_j(Y_t)\|b_j(Y_t)-y_j(t)\|^2 \le 0.dtd​F(Yt​)=−2j∑​aj​(Yt​)∥bj​(Yt​)−yj​(t)∥2≤0.
(30)

If μt=∑jwjδyj(t)\mu_t=\sum_j w_j\delta_{y_j(t)}μt​=∑j​wj​δyj​(t)​ carries fixed positive weights, independent of the Voronoi masses aj(Yt)a_j(Y_t)aj​(Yt​), this labelled particle ODE is equivalently a weak continuity equation,

∂tμt+div⁡(vμtμt)=0,vμt(yj(t))=bj(Yt)−yj(t),\partial_t\mu_t+\operatorname{div}(v_{\mu_t}\mu_t)=0, \qquad v_{\mu_t}(y_j(t))=b_j(Y_t)-y_j(t),∂t​μt​+div(vμt​​μt​)=0,vμt​​(yj​(t))=bj​(Yt​)−yj​(t),
(31)

in the sense of the measure evolutions introduced in Chapter Paragraph. The weights wjw_jwj​ in this transport equation are auxiliary weights for the moving labelled particles; they are not the Voronoi masses used to define the quantization energy. If one records instead the free-weight projection

νYt=∑jaj(Yt)δyj(t),\nu_{Y_t}=\sum_j a_j(Y_t)\delta_{y_j(t)},νYt​​=j∑​aj​(Yt​)δyj​(t)​,
(32)

then, formally,

∂tνYt+div⁡(vtνYt)=∑ja˙j(Yt)δyj(t),vt(yj(t))=y˙j(t).\partial_t\nu_{Y_t}+\operatorname{div}(v_t\nu_{Y_t}) = \sum_j\dot a_j(Y_t)\delta_{y_j(t)}, \qquad v_t(y_j(t))=\dot y_j(t).∂t​νYt​​+div(vt​νYt​​)=j∑​a˙j​(Yt​)δyj​(t)​,vt​(yj​(t))=y˙​j​(t).
(33)

Thus the free-weight quantizer evolves by a balance equation, not by pure transport. This is why the construction is intrinsically finite-dimensional: Voronoi cells, centroids and labels define the velocity, and a canonical extension to arbitrary measures is not obtained by replacing YYY with the support of a measure. Indeed, any measure with dense support would have zero support-distance quantization error.

Mean-Field Limit and Ultrafast Diffusion¶

There is nevertheless a precise high-resolution continuum theory when the number MMM of codepoints tends to infinity. If α=ρ  dx\alpha=\rho\,\d xα=ρdx, the limiting Eulerian variable is the density fff of sites, meaning heuristically that Mf(x)  dxM f(x)\,\d xMf(x)dx codepoints lie in  dx\d xdx. Thus the limit is M→+∞M\to+\inftyM→+∞, not a limit in the exponent of the PDE. In one dimension, Caglioti, Golse and Iacobelli embed the ordered particle configuration in L2(0,1)L^2(0,1)L2(0,1) and prove quantitative convergence of the discrete gradient flow toward a limiting flow Caglioti et al., 2015. A perturbative two-dimensional analysis around the optimal hexagonal lattice is developed in Caglioti et al., 2018. For ppp-quantization in dimension ddd, set r=p/dr=p/dr=p/d, so r=2/dr=2/dr=2/d for the quadratic cost used in this section. The rescaled local energy MrQM(α)pM^r\mathcal Q_M(\alpha)^pMrQM​(α)p is described, up to the universal cell-shape constant, by

Gρ(f):=∫Ωρ(x)f(x)−r  dx,∫Ωf  dx=1,\mathcal G_\rho(f) \eqdef \int_\Omega \rho(x) f(x)^{-r}\,\d x, \qquad \int_\Omega f\,\d x=1,Gρ​(f):=∫Ω​ρ(x)f(x)−rdx,∫Ω​fdx=1,
(34)

and its formal W2\mathcal W_2W2​-gradient flow is the weighted ultrafast diffusion equation

∂tf=−r div⁡ ⁣(f∇ ⁣(ρfr+1)),\partial_t f = -r\,\operatorname{div}\!\left( f\nabla\!\left(\frac{\rho}{f^{r+1}}\right) \right),∂t​f=−rdiv(f∇(fr+1ρ​)),
(35)

with periodic or no-flux boundary conditions. Iacobelli studies the associated one-dimensional very-fast-diffusion equation and its convergence to equilibrium Iacobelli, 2019; Iacobelli, Patacchini and Santambrogio then use the JKO scheme and Wasserstein-gradient-flow tools to prove well-posedness, regularity estimates and convergence for a multidimensional weighted version Iacobelli et al., 2019. With ω=ρ1/(r+1)\omega=\rho^{1/(r+1)}ω=ρ1/(r+1) and u=f/ωu=f/\omegau=f/ω, the same equation becomes

∂tu=−r+1ωdiv⁡ ⁣(ω∇(u−r)),\partial_t u = -\frac{r+1}{\omega}\operatorname{div}\!\left(\omega\nabla(u^{-r})\right),∂t​u=−ωr+1​div(ω∇(u−r)),
(36)

which makes the negative exponent, hence the ultrafast-diffusion character, explicit.

show_book_figure("semidiscrete-lloyd-flow-mixtures")

Relaxed Lloyd flow from a source Gaussian-mixture initialization toward a different target Gaussian-mixture density. The blue contours and shading show the target density α\alphaα, while the colored disks are the moving codepoints initialized from the source mixture. The faint curves trace the labelled sites under the explicit-Euler Lloyd ODE. The right panel displays the relative quantization energy, illustrating the monotone decay of the objective along the relaxed iterations.

show_book_figure("semidiscrete-lloyd-quantization")

Lloyd quantization for the same continuous density and twenty-one initial sites as the Laguerre-cell figure. The red contours show the density α\alphaα, while the colored disks are the current codepoints and have the same colors as their Voronoi cells. The iterations move the initially left-located sites toward the high-density region and reshape the cells according to centroidal Voronoi geometry.

The interactive demo separates the nonconvex geometry from the fixed-point update: increase the iteration counter and watch sites migrate toward the density before settling into a local centroidal configuration.

Interactive panel. Use the iteration and site controls to compare Lloyd-style quantization steps with the semi-discrete geometry.

Algorithm: Lloyd quantization

Input: Source measure α\alphaα, initial codepoints Y(0)=(yj(0))j=1mY^{(0)}=(y_j^{(0)})_{j=1}^mY(0)=(yj(0)​)j=1m​, squared Euclidean cost, tolerance tol\mathrm{tol}tol.

Output: Codepoints Y=(yj)j=1mY=(y_j)_{j=1}^mY=(yj​)j=1m​.

Initialize: Set d0=+∞d_0=+\inftyd0​=+∞ and k=0k=0k=0.

While dk>told_k>\mathrm{tol}dk​>tol do:

Set k←k+1k\leftarrow k+1k←k+1.

Compute Voronoi cells: Vj(Y(k−1))={x  :  c(x,yj(k−1))≤c(x,yℓ(k−1))∀ℓ}.\VV_j(Y^{(k-1)}) = \enscond{x}{c(x,y_j^{(k-1)})\leq c(x,y_\ell^{(k-1)})\quad\forall \ell}.Vj​(Y(k−1))={x:c(x,yj(k−1)​)≤c(x,yℓ(k−1)​)∀ℓ}.

For each nonempty cell Vj\VV_jVj​ do

yj(k)=∫Vj(Y(k−1))x  dα(x)∫Vj(Y(k−1)) dα(x).y_j^{(k)} = \frac{\int_{\VV_j(Y^{(k-1)})}x\,\d\al(x)} {\int_{\VV_j(Y^{(k-1)})}\d\al(x)}.yj(k)​=∫Vj​(Y(k−1))​dα(x)∫Vj​(Y(k−1))​xdα(x)​.

For each empty cell Vj\VV_jVj​ do:

Set yj(k)=yj(k−1)y_j^{(k)}=y_j^{(k-1)}yj(k)​=yj(k−1)​.

Set dk=max⁡j∥yj(k)−yj(k−1)∥d_k=\max_j\norm{y_j^{(k)}-y_j^{(k-1)}}dk​=maxj​∥∥​yj(k)​−yj(k−1)​∥∥​.

Return Y(k)Y^{(k)}Y(k).

Quantization with Fixed Equal Weights¶

The free-mass formulation above optimizes the positions and the weights of the atoms. A different problem is obtained by prescribing the weights. In the equal-weight case, set

νY:=1m∑j=1mδyj,Feq(Y):=12W22(α,νY),\nu_Y\eqdef \frac1m\sum_{j=1}^m\delta_{y_j}, \qquad \mathcal F_{\rm eq}(Y) \eqdef \frac12\mathcal W_2^2(\alpha,\nu_Y),νY​:=m1​j=1∑m​δyj​​,Feq​(Y):=21​W22​(α,νY​),
(37)

and minimize only over the positions Y=(yj)jY=(y_j)_jY=(yj​)j​. Assume that α\alphaα has a density and that the semi-discrete optimal cells are well defined. Let Cj(Y)C_j(Y)Cj​(Y) be the Laguerre cell transported to yjy_jyj​, so that α(Cj(Y))=1/m\alpha(C_j(Y))=1/mα(Cj​(Y))=1/m, and define its centroid

xˉj(Y):=m∫Cj(Y)x  dα(x).\bar x_j(Y) \eqdef m\int_{C_j(Y)} x\,\d\alpha(x).xˉj​(Y):=m∫Cj​(Y)​xdα(x).
(38)

At differentiability points, the envelope theorem gives

∇yjFeq(Y)=1m(yj−xˉj(Y)).\nabla_{y_j}\mathcal F_{\rm eq}(Y) = \frac1m\bigl(y_j-\bar x_j(Y)\bigr).∇yj​​Feq​(Y)=m1​(yj​−xˉj​(Y)).
(39)

The W2\mathcal W_2W2​ metric on equal-weight empirical measures induces the particle metric gY(U,V)=m−1∑j⟨uj,vj⟩g_Y(U,V)=m^{-1}\sum_j\langle u_j,v_j\ranglegY​(U,V)=m−1∑j​⟨uj​,vj​⟩. Hence the associated W2\mathcal W_2W2​ gradient flow is the coupled system

y˙j(t)=xˉj(Yt)−yj(t),j=1,…,m.\dot y_j(t) = \bar x_j(Y_t)-y_j(t), \qquad j=1,\ldots,m.y˙​j​(t)=xˉj​(Yt​)−yj​(t),j=1,…,m.
(40)

Equivalently, νYt\nu_{Y_t}νYt​​ satisfies a continuity equation with velocity vt(yj(t))=xˉj(Yt)−yj(t)v_t(y_j(t))=\bar x_j(Y_t)-y_j(t)vt​(yj​(t))=xˉj​(Yt​)−yj​(t). This is the so-called finite-particle W2\mathcal W_2W2​ gradient-flow viewpoint developed more systematically in Chapter Paragraph; the fixed-weight cells are Laguerre cells rather than the free-mass Voronoi cells used by Lloyd’s method.

Equal-weight quantization on the line¶

The following classical scalar quantization result gives the precise form of the inverse-CDF rule for equal-weight quadratic quantization Graf & Luschgy, 2000. The atoms are not exactly the midpoint quantiles in general; they are the averages of the quantile function over equal mass bins. Midpoint inverse-CDF samples are nevertheless asymptotically equivalent and are often the most convenient rule in numerical examples.

Proposition: One-Dimensional Equal-Weight Quantization

Let α∈M+1(R)\alpha\in\mathcal M_+^1(\mathbb R)α∈M+1​(R) have finite second moment and quantile function Q=Fα−1Q=F_\alpha^{-1}Q=Fα−1​. For

Qm,eq(α)2:=min⁡y1≤⋯≤ymW22(α,1m∑i=1mδyi),\mathcal Q_{m,\mathrm{eq}}(\alpha)^2 \eqdef \min_{y_1\le\cdots\le y_m} \mathcal W_2^2 \left(\alpha,\frac1m\sum_{i=1}^m\delta_{y_i}\right),Qm,eq​(α)2:=y1​≤⋯≤ym​min​W22​(α,m1​i=1∑m​δyi​​),
(41)

set Ii=((i−1)/m,i/m]I_i=((i-1)/m,i/m]Ii​=((i−1)/m,i/m]. Then the sorted minimizer is unique and its iiith atom is

yi⋆=m∫IiQ(u)  du,y_i^\star = m\int_{I_i} Q(u)\,\d u,yi⋆​=m∫Ii​​Q(u)du,
(42)

and

Qm,eq(α)2=∑i=1m∫Ii∣Q(u)−yi⋆∣2  du.\mathcal Q_{m,\mathrm{eq}}(\alpha)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^m \int_{I_i} \left|Q(u)-y_i^\star\right|^2\,\d u.Qm,eq​(α)2=i=1∑m​∫Ii​​∣Q(u)−yi⋆​∣2du.
(43)

If Q∈C1([0,1])Q\in C^1([0,1])Q∈C1([0,1]), then

m2Qm,eq(α)2⟶112∫01∣Q′(u)∣2  du.m^2\mathcal Q_{m,\mathrm{eq}}(\alpha)^2 \longrightarrow \frac1{12}\int_0^1 |Q'(u)|^2\,\d u.m2Qm,eq​(α)2⟶121​∫01​∣Q′(u)∣2du.
(44)
Proof

After sorting the atoms, the quantile formula for W2\mathcal W_2W2​ gives

W22(α,1m∑i=1mδyi)=∑i=1m∫Ii∣Q(u)−yi∣2  du.\mathcal W_2^2 \left(\alpha,\frac1m\sum_{i=1}^m\delta_{y_i}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^m \int_{I_i}|Q(u)-y_i|^2\,\d u.W22​(α,m1​i=1∑m​δyi​​)=i=1∑m​∫Ii​​∣Q(u)−yi​∣2du.
(45)

The minimization therefore decouples over the intervals IiI_iIi​, and the best constant approximation of QQQ on IiI_iIi​ in L2L^2L2 is its average on IiI_iIi​. This proves the formula for yi⋆y_i^\staryi⋆​ and the energy.

Denote by QˉIi=m∫IiQ(u)  du\bar Q_{I_i}=m\int_{I_i}Q(u)\,\d uQˉ​Ii​​=m∫Ii​​Q(u)du the interval average. If QQQ is C1C^1C1, set h=1/mh=1/mh=1/m and write Ii=(ai,ai+h]I_i=(a_i,a_i+h]Ii​=(ai​,ai​+h]. Uniform Taylor expansion gives, for v∈[0,1]v\in[0,1]v∈[0,1],

Q(ai+hv)=Q(ai)+hvQ′(ai)+h ri(v),max⁡isup⁡v∈[0,1]∣ri(v)∣→0.Q(a_i+hv) = Q(a_i)+hvQ'(a_i)+h\,r_i(v), \qquad \max_i\sup_{v\in[0,1]} |r_i(v)|\to0.Q(ai​+hv)=Q(ai​)+hvQ′(ai​)+hri​(v),imax​v∈[0,1]sup​∣ri​(v)∣→0.
(46)

Subtracting the average over v∈[0,1]v\in[0,1]v∈[0,1] and integrating gives

∫Ii∣Q(u)−QˉIi∣2  du=h312∣Q′(ai)∣2+o(h3),\int_{I_i}|Q(u)-\bar Q_{I_i}|^2\,\d u = \frac{h^3}{12}|Q'(a_i)|^2+o(h^3),∫Ii​​∣Q(u)−Qˉ​Ii​​∣2du=12h3​∣Q′(ai​)∣2+o(h3),
(47)

uniformly in iii. Summing over iii yields a Riemann sum for 112∫01∣Q′(u)∣2  du\frac1{12}\int_0^1 |Q'(u)|^2\,\d u121​∫01​∣Q′(u)∣2du.

Thus the common deterministic rule m−1∑iδQ((i−1/2)/m)m^{-1}\sum_i\delta_{Q((i-1/2)/m)}m−1∑i​δQ((i−1/2)/m)​ should be read as a midpoint approximation of the optimal bin-average formula. It has the same leading squared error under the same smoothness assumptions; for the uniform law on [0,1][0,1][0,1], both rules coincide and give the regular grid yi=(i−1/2)/my_i=(i-1/2)/myi​=(i−1/2)/m. Random sampling has a different asymptotic regime.

Proposition: Quantile-Process Asymptotics for Random Placement

Let α∈M+1(R)\alpha\in\mathcal M_+^1(\mathbb R)α∈M+1​(R) have quantile Q∈C1([0,1])Q\in C^1([0,1])Q∈C1([0,1]), and let α^m=m−1∑i=1mδXi\widehat\alpha_m=m^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^m\delta_{X_i}αm​=m−1∑i=1m​δXi​​ with XiX_iXi​ iid with law α\alphaα. If BBB denotes the standard Brownian bridge on [0,1][0,1][0,1], then

m W22(α,α^m)⟶law∫01B(u)2∣Q′(u)∣2  du,m\,\mathcal W_2^2(\alpha,\widehat\alpha_m) \overset{\mathrm{law}}{\longrightarrow} \int_0^1 B(u)^2 |Q'(u)|^2\,\d u,mW22​(α,αm​)⟶law​∫01​B(u)2∣Q′(u)∣2du,
(48)

and, in expectation,

m E ⁣[W22(α,α^m)]⟶∫01u(1−u)∣Q′(u)∣2  du.m\,\mathbb E\!\left[ \mathcal W_2^2(\alpha,\widehat\alpha_m) \right] \longrightarrow \int_0^1 u(1-u)|Q'(u)|^2\,\d u.mE[W22​(α,αm​)]⟶∫01​u(1−u)∣Q′(u)∣2du.
(49)
Proof

Let Q^m\widehat Q_mQ​m​ be the empirical quantile function. The one-dimensional formula gives

W22(α,α^m)=∫01∣Q^m(u)−Q(u)∣2  du.\mathcal W_2^2(\alpha,\widehat\alpha_m) = \int_0^1|\widehat Q_m(u)-Q(u)|^2\,\d u.W22​(α,αm​)=∫01​∣Q​m​(u)−Q(u)∣2du.
(50)

Write Xi=Q(Ui)X_i=Q(U_i)Xi​=Q(Ui​) with UiU_iUi​ iid uniform on [0,1][0,1][0,1], and let U^m−1\widehat U_m^{-1}Um−1​ be the empirical quantile function of the UiU_iUi​. Then Q^m=Q∘U^m−1\widehat Q_m=Q\circ \widehat U_m^{-1}Q​m​=Q∘Um−1​. The classical uniform quantile-process theorem Vaart & Wellner, 1996 gives

m (U^m−1−Id⁡)⟶law−Bin L2(0,1).\sqrt m\,(\widehat U_m^{-1}-\operatorname{Id}) \overset{\mathrm{law}}{\longrightarrow} -B \quad\text{in }L^2(0,1).m​(Um−1​−Id)⟶law​−Bin L2(0,1).
(51)

Since Q∈C1([0,1])Q\in C^1([0,1])Q∈C1([0,1]), the map u↦Q(u)u\mapsto Q(u)u↦Q(u) is Hadamard differentiable along these perturbations, and therefore

m (Q^m−Q)⟶law−BQ′in L2(0,1).\sqrt m\,(\widehat Q_m-Q) \overset{\mathrm{law}}{\longrightarrow} -BQ' \quad\text{in }L^2(0,1).m​(Q​m​−Q)⟶law​−BQ′in L2(0,1).
(52)

Since BBB and −B-B−B have the same law, the sign disappears in the squared L2L^2L2 norm. The continuous mapping theorem gives the distributional convergence. Standard second-moment bounds for the uniform quantile process, together with the boundedness of Q′Q'Q′, give uniform integrability, hence convergence of expectations. Since E[B(u)2]=u(1−u)\mathbb E[B(u)^2]=u(1-u)E[B(u)2]=u(1−u), Fubini’s theorem gives the displayed expectation limit.

Combining these propositions gives a sharp contrast between optimal placement and random placement on the line. Deterministic equal-weight quantization has squared error of order m−2m^{-2}m−2, hence W2\mathcal W_2W2​ error of order m−1m^{-1}m−1, while iid empirical sampling has average squared error of order m−1m^{-1}m−1, hence root-mean-square W2\mathcal W_2W2​ error of order m−1/2m^{-1/2}m−1/2. This is consistent with broader empirical OT sample-complexity theory Dereich et al., 2013Fournier & Guillin, 2015Weed & Bach, 2019.

show_book_figure("semidiscrete-quantile-quantization-rates")

One-dimensional equal-weight quantization in quantile coordinates. Left: for a smooth positive density on [0,1][0,1][0,1], the colored atoms are bin averages of the inverse CDF over equal quantile intervals, while the gray atoms show one iid empirical draw with the same number of particles. Right: average squared W2\mathcal W_2W2​ errors. The deterministic bin averages and midpoint quantiles follow the m−2m^{-2}m−2 squared-error law, whereas iid empirical measures follow the slower m−1m^{-1}m−1 average squared-error law.

Interactive panel. Change the one-dimensional law, the number of atoms and the Monte Carlo seed to compare optimal equal-weight quantization with random empirical sampling. The right panel recomputes the squared W2\Wass_2W2​ error curves from the quantile formula.

Remark: Sparse LP and network simplex

Let N=∣V∣N=|V|N=∣V∣ and M=∣E∣M=|E|M=∣E∣. The graph Beckmann problem is a linear program, but it is much smaller than the dense Kantorovich LP on the same vertex set. Indeed, writing m=m+−m−m=m^+-m^-m=m+−m− gives

min⁡m+,m−≥0∑e∈Eℓe(me++me−)subject todiv⁡G(m+−m−)=r.\min_{m^+,m^-\geq0}\sum_{e\in E}\ell_e(m^+_e+m^-_e) \quad\text{subject to}\quad \operatorname{div}_G(m^+-m^-)=r .m+,m−≥0min​e∈E∑​ℓe​(me+​+me−​)subject todivG​(m+−m−)=r.
(53)

This formulation has 2M2M2M nonnegative variables and N−1N-1N−1 independent balance constraints, whereas the standard transport LP between two measures on VVV has N2N^2N2 coupling variables and 2N−12N-12N−1 independent marginal constraints. For sparse geometric graphs, such as planar Delaunay graphs where typically M=O(N)M=O(N)M=O(N), the graph formulation is therefore linear-size rather than quadratic-size.

The same LP is a minimum-cost transshipment problem. Replace each undirected edge {i,j}\{i,j\}{i,j} by the two directed arcs i→ji\to ji→j and j→ij\to ij→i, both with cost ℓe\ell_eℓe​, and impose the node balances ∑juij−∑juji=ri\sum_{j}u_{ij}-\sum_j u_{ji}=r_i∑j​uij​−∑j​uji​=ri​. This is exactly the setting of the network simplex method: a basis is a spanning tree, a pivot adds one non-tree arc, creates a unique cycle, sends flow along this cycle, and updates the node potentials and reduced costs Bertsekas & Eckstein, 1988Orlin, 1997. A basic implementation needs O(M)O(M)O(M) work to price all arcs and O(N)O(N)O(N) work to update the tree at each pivot, hence O(PM)O(PM)O(PM) arithmetic operations for PPP pivots on a sparse graph. The pivot count PPP depends on the rule and can be large in worst-case simplex analyses, but network-simplex variants and general minimum-cost-flow algorithms give polynomial guarantees in NNN and MMM; in practice, this edge-based formulation is often far cheaper than solving the dense N2N^2N2-variable transport LP.

Algorithm: Graph Beckmann network-simplex pivot

Input: Graph G=(V,E)G=(V,E)G=(V,E), edge lengths ℓe\ell_eℓe​, node balances rir_iri​ with ∑iri=0\sum_ir_i=0∑i​ri​=0.

Output: Minimum-cost graph flow uuu.

Replace each undirected edge by two directed arcs.

Impose balances: ∑juij−∑juji=ri.\sum_j u_{ij}-\sum_j u_{ji}=r_i .∑j​uij​−∑j​uji​=ri​.

Initialize: Add artificial root arcs and compute a feasible tree flow on a spanning tree TTT with node potentials.

While min⁡e∉Tcˉe<0\min_{e\notin T}\bar c_e<0mine∈/T​cˉe​<0 do:

Set entering arc eee to the first minimizer of cˉa\bar c_acˉa​ over a∉Ta\notin Ta∈/T in the prescribed arc order.

Add it to the tree.

Set C=\mathcal C=C= unique induced cycle, oriented in the direction of the entering arc eee.

Set θ=min⁡{ua: a∈C−}\theta=\min\{u_a:\ a\in\mathcal C^-\}θ=min{ua​: a∈C−}, where C−\mathcal C^-C− are the arcs opposed to the cycle orientation.

Update ua←ua+θu_a\leftarrow u_a+\thetaua​←ua​+θ for a∈C+a\in\mathcal C^+a∈C+ and ua←ua−θu_a\leftarrow u_a-\thetaua​←ua​−θ for a∈C−a\in\mathcal C^-a∈C−.

Remove the first arc in C−\mathcal C^-C− attaining the minimum θ\thetaθ, using the prescribed cycle order.

Update the tree, potentials, and reduced costs.

Return uuu.

Wasserstein-1 Norm¶

The W1\Wass_1W1​ distance has an especially transparent dual: the admissible potentials are exactly 1-Lipschitz test functions. This makes W1\Wass_1W1​ the meeting point between transport, PDE formulations and weak norms on signed measures.

c-Transform for Wasserstein-1¶

Assume that ddd is a distance on X=Y\X=\YX=Y and take the ground cost c(x,y)=d(x,y)c(x,y)=d(x,y)c(x,y)=d(x,y).

Definition: Lipschitz Constant

For a function f:X→Rf:\X\to\RRf:X→R on a metric space (X,d)(\X,d)(X,d), its Lipschitz constant is

Lip⁡(f):=sup⁡x≠y∣f(x)−f(y)∣d(x,y).\Lip(f) \eqdef \sup_{x\ne y} \frac{|f(x)-f(y)|}{d(x,y)}.Lip(f):=x=ysup​d(x,y)∣f(x)−f(y)∣​.
(54)

The function is 1-Lipschitz when Lip⁡(f)≤1\Lip(f)\le1Lip(f)≤1.

Proposition: ccc-Transforms and 1-Lipschitz Functions

Suppose X=Y\X=\YX=Y and c(x,y)=d(x,y)c(x,y)=d(x,y)c(x,y)=d(x,y). Then there exists ggg such that f=gcf=g^cf=gc if and only if Lip⁡(f)≤1\Lip(f)\le1Lip(f)≤1. Furthermore, if Lip⁡(f)≤1\Lip(f)\le1Lip(f)≤1, then fc=−ff^c=-ffc=−f.

Proof

First suppose f=gcf=g^cf=gc for some ggg. For x,y∈Xx,y\in\Xx,y∈X,

∣f(x)−f(y)∣=∣inf⁡z[d(x,z)−g(z)]−inf⁡z[d(y,z)−g(z)]∣≤sup⁡z∣d(x,z)−d(y,z)∣≤d(x,y),|f(x)-f(y)| = \left| \inf_z [d(x,z)-g(z)] - \inf_z [d(y,z)-g(z)] \right| \le \sup_z |d(x,z)-d(y,z)| \le d(x,y),∣f(x)−f(y)∣=∣∣​zinf​[d(x,z)−g(z)]−zinf​[d(y,z)−g(z)]∣∣​≤zsup​∣d(x,z)−d(y,z)∣≤d(x,y),
(55)

where the last inequality is the reverse triangle inequality. Thus Lip⁡(f)≤1\Lip(f)\le1Lip(f)≤1.

If Lip⁡(f)≤1\Lip(f)\le1Lip(f)≤1, then f(x)≤f(y)+d(x,y)f(x)\le f(y)+d(x,y)f(x)≤f(y)+d(x,y), so d(x,y)−f(x)≥−f(y)d(x,y)-f(x)\ge -f(y)d(x,y)−f(x)≥−f(y) for all xxx, hence fc(y)≥−f(y)f^c(y)\ge -f(y)fc(y)≥−f(y). Taking x=yx=yx=y gives fc(y)≤−f(y)f^c(y)\le -f(y)fc(y)≤−f(y). Therefore fc=−ff^c=-ffc=−f. Applying the same property to −f-f−f gives (−f)c=f(-f)^c=f(−f)c=f, so every 1-Lipschitz function is ccc-concave.

Using the alternating ccc-transform scheme from the dual chapter, one can replace the dual pair by (f,−f)(f,-f)(f,−f) with Lip⁡(f)≤1\Lip(f)\le1Lip(f)≤1. The Kantorovich dual therefore becomes the Kantorovich--Rubinstein formula

W1(α,β)=max⁡f{∫Xf  d(α−β):Lip⁡(f)≤1}=:∥α−β∥W1.\Wass_1(\alpha,\beta) = \max_f \left\{ \int_\X f\,\d(\alpha-\beta) : \Lip(f)\le1 \right\} =: \norm{\alpha-\beta}_{W_1}.W1​(α,β)=fmax​{∫X​fd(α−β):Lip(f)≤1}=:∥α−β∥W1​​.
(56)

This expression depends only on the signed measure ξ=α−β\xi=\alpha-\betaξ=α−β. It therefore extends to every finite signed measure ξ\xiξ with ξ(X)=0\xi(\X)=0ξ(X)=0 by the same supremum, and defines the Kantorovich--Rubinstein norm ∥⋅∥W1\norm{\cdot}_{W_1}∥⋅∥W1​​ on that space Kantorovich & Rubinstein, 1958.

For a discrete signed measure α−β=∑krkδzk\alpha-\beta=\sum_k r_k\delta_{z_k}α−β=∑k​rk​δzk​​ with ∑krk=0\sum_k r_k=0∑k​rk​=0,

W1(α,β)=max⁡(fk)k{∑kfkrk:∣fk−fℓ∣≤d(zk,zℓ)for all k,ℓ}.\Wass_1(\alpha,\beta) = \max_{(f_k)_k} \left\{ \sum_k f_k r_k : |f_k-f_\ell|\le d(z_k,z_\ell) \quad\text{for all }k,\ell \right\}.W1​(α,β)=(fk​)k​max​{k∑​fk​rk​:∣fk​−fℓ​∣≤d(zk​,zℓ​)for all k,ℓ}.
(57)

This finite-dimensional linear program can be solved by generic interior-point or first-order methods. If NNN support points are involved, however, it still contains O(N2)O(N^2)O(N2) Lipschitz constraints, mirroring the O(nm)O(nm)O(nm) coupling variables of the original discrete Kantorovich LP; the dual formulation alone does not remove the all-pairs structure. The gain comes on structured metric spaces where the distance is generated locally: it is then enough to impose Lipschitz inequalities on neighboring pairs, because summing along paths recovers the constraints between arbitrary points. The one-dimensional ordered case is the first example; the graph-geodesic case is described later in Proposition Proposition: W1\Wass_1W1​ and Beckmann Flow on a Graph.

When d(x,y)=∣x−y∣d(x,y)=|x-y|d(x,y)=∣x−y∣ on R\RRR, ordering the support points z1≤z2≤⋯z_1\le z_2\le\cdotsz1​≤z2​≤⋯ reduces the constraints to neighboring pairs:

W1(α,β)=max⁡(fk)k{∑kfkrk:∣fk+1−fk∣≤zk+1−zkfor all k}.\Wass_1(\alpha,\beta) = \max_{(f_k)_k} \left\{ \sum_k f_k r_k : |f_{k+1}-f_k|\le z_{k+1}-z_k \quad\text{for all }k \right\}.W1​(α,β)=(fk​)k​max​{k∑​fk​rk​:∣fk+1​−fk​∣≤zk+1​−zk​for all k}.
(58)

In one dimension this is equivalent to the closed-form cumulative formula introduced earlier.

Wasserstein-1 on Euclidean Spaces¶

For X=Y=Rd\X=\Y=\RR^dX=Y=Rd with c(x,y)=∥x−y∥c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}c(x,y)=∥x−y∥, the global Lipschitz constraint in the Kantorovich--Rubinstein formula can be made local as a uniform bound on the gradient:

W1(α,β)=sup⁡f{∫Rdf ( dα− dβ):∥∇f∥∞≤1}.\Wass_1(\alpha,\beta) = \sup_f \left\{ \int_{\RR^d} f\,(\d\alpha-\d\beta) : \norm{\nabla f}_\infty\le1 \right\}.W1​(α,β)=fsup​{∫Rd​f(dα−dβ):∥∇f∥∞​≤1}.
(59)

Dualizing this expression with respect to vector fields m:Rd→Rdm:\RR^d\to\RR^dm:Rd→Rd gives the fixed-divergence problem

W1(α,β)=inf⁡m{∫Rd∥m(x)∥  dx:div⁡(m)=α−β},\Wass_1(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_m \left\{ \int_{\RR^d}\norm{m(x)}\,\d x : \operatorname{div}(m)=\alpha-\beta \right\},W1​(α,β)=minf​{∫Rd​∥m(x)∥dx:div(m)=α−β},
(60)

often called the Beckmann formulation Beckmann, 1952. The vector field m(x)m(x)m(x) describes local movement of mass. Outside the support of the two input measures, div⁡(m)=0\operatorname{div}(m)=0div(m)=0, which is conservation of mass.

Once discretized with finite elements, the dual Lipschitz problem and the Beckmann problem become nonsmooth convex optimization problems. The same formulation extends to Riemannian manifolds by replacing the Euclidean distance by geodesic distance and interpreting gradient and divergence as differential operators on the manifold.

Graph Distances and Beckmann Flows¶

Finite graphs give a simple discrete instance where a metric is generated by local moves, so the all-pairs Lipschitz constraints collapse to edge constraints.

Definition: Graph Geodesic Distance

Let G=(V,E)G=(V,E)G=(V,E) be a connected finite graph with positive edge lengths (ℓe)e∈E(\ell_e)_{e\in E}(ℓe​)e∈E​. The graph geodesic distance between two vertices is

dG(i,j)=min⁡γ:i⇝j∑e∈γℓe.d_G(i,j) = \min_{\gamma:i\leadsto j} \sum_{e\in\gamma}\ell_e .dG​(i,j)=γ:i⇝jmin​e∈γ∑​ℓe​.
(61)

The minimum is over all paths γ\gammaγ joining iii to jjj.

This graph distance turns W1\Wass_1W1​ into a finite-dimensional flow problem.

Proposition: W1\Wass_1W1​ and Beckmann Flow on a Graph

Let G=(V,E)G=(V,E)G=(V,E) be a connected finite graph with positive edge lengths (ℓe)e∈E(\ell_e)_{e\in E}(ℓe​)e∈E​ and graph geodesic distance dGd_GdG​. For probability vectors a,ba,ba,b on VVV, set r=a−br=a-br=a−b and orient each edge e=(i,j)e=(i,j)e=(i,j). If

(∇Gf)e=fj−fi,div⁡G=−∇G∗(\nabla_G f)_e=f_j-f_i, \qquad \operatorname{div}_G=-\nabla_G^*(∇G​f)e​=fj​−fi​,divG​=−∇G∗​
(62)

are the finite-difference gradient and negative adjoint, then

W1,G(a,b)=max⁡f{∑i∈Vfiri:∣fi−fj∣≤ℓe∀e=(i,j)}=min⁡m{∑e∈Eℓe∣me∣:div⁡Gm=r}.\Wass_{1,G}(a,b) = \max_f \left\{ \sum_{i\in V} f_i r_i : |f_i-f_j|\le\ell_e \quad\forall e=(i,j) \right\} = \min_m \left\{ \sum_{e\in E}\ell_e |m_e| : \operatorname{div}_G m=r \right\}.W1,G​(a,b)=fmax​{i∈V∑​fi​ri​:∣fi​−fj​∣≤ℓe​∀e=(i,j)}=mmin​{e∈E∑​ℓe​∣me​∣:divG​m=r}.
(63)

The vector mem_eme​ is an oriented edge flow, and the constraint div⁡Gm=r\operatorname{div}_G m=rdivG​m=r is conservation of mass at each vertex.

Proof

The edge constraint ∣fi−fj∣≤ℓe|f_i-f_j|\le\ell_e∣fi​−fj​∣≤ℓe​ implies, by summing along paths, that ∣fi−fj∣≤dG(i,j)|f_i-f_j|\le d_G(i,j)∣fi​−fj​∣≤dG​(i,j) for all vertices. Conversely, any 1-Lipschitz function for dGd_GdG​ satisfies the edge constraints because each edge is a path of length ℓe\ell_eℓe​. The first equality is therefore the Kantorovich--Rubinstein formula on the metric space (V,dG)(V,d_G)(V,dG​).

For the second equality, write the graph Beckmann problem and dualize its equality constraint with a potential fff:

inf⁡m∑eℓe∣me∣+sup⁡f∑ifi(ri−(div⁡Gm)i).\inf_m \sum_e \ell_e|m_e| + \sup_f \sum_i f_i \left(r_i-(\operatorname{div}_G m)_i\right).minf​e∑​ℓe​∣me​∣+fsup​i∑​fi​(ri​−(divG​m)i​).
(64)

Using div⁡G=−∇G∗\operatorname{div}_G=-\nabla_G^*divG​=−∇G∗​, the coupling term is ∑eme(∇Gf)e\sum_e m_e(\nabla_G f)_e∑e​me​(∇G​f)e​. The minimization over each scalar flow mem_eme​ is finite exactly when ∣(∇Gf)e∣≤ℓe|(\nabla_G f)_e|\le\ell_e∣(∇G​f)e​∣≤ℓe​, and is then equal to zero. The dual problem is the graph Lipschitz dual above. Strong duality holds because this is a finite-dimensional linear program with a nonempty feasible set: connectedness and ∑iri=0\sum_i r_i=0∑i​ri​=0 allow the signed surplus to be routed along paths.

show_book_figure("w1-graph-transport-flow")

Graph Beckmann formulation of W1\Wass_1W1​ on a Delaunay graph. Red and blue disks encode the positive and negative parts of r=α−βr=\alpha-\betar=α−β. Violet arrows display the signed edge flow mmm: orientation gives the sign, width is proportional to ∣me∣\sqrt{|m_e|}∣me​∣​, and the flow satisfies the conservation constraint div⁡Gm=r\operatorname{div}_G m=rdivG​m=r.

The interactive graph view lets the source and sink clusters move and changes the graph resolution. It makes the transshipment interpretation of W1\Wass_1W1​ visible: signed mass is routed through local edges rather than matched only by straight source-to-target segments.

Interactive panel. Use the graph and demand controls to inspect how Wasserstein-1 transport becomes a flow problem on edges.

This graph formulation is the transshipment version of W1\Wass_1W1​. It is the natural discrete analogue of the Beckmann formulation: gradients are edge differences, divergences are incidence-matrix balances, and geodesic distance is shortest-path length. It can be solved by min-cost flow methods on sparse graphs, while entropic or KL-projection variants lead to flow-Sinkhorn algorithms for graph W1\Wass_1W1​ Beckmann, 1952Peyré, 2026.

References¶
  1. Aurenhammer, F., Hoffmann, F., & Aronov, B. (1998). Minkowski-type theorems and least-squares clustering. Algorithmica, 20(1), 61–76.
  2. Mérigot, Q. (2011). A multiscale approach to optimal transport. Computer Graphics Forum, 30(5), 1583–1592.
  3. Mérigot, Q. (2013). A comparison of two dual methods for discrete optimal transport. In Geometric science of information (pp. 389–396). Springer.
  4. Kantorovich, L., & Rubinstein, G. S. (1958). On a space of totally additive functions. Vestn Leningrad Universitet, 13, 52–59.
  5. Beckmann, M. (1952). A continuous model of transportation. Econometrica, 20, 643–660.
  6. Aurenhammer, F. (1987). Power diagrams: properties, algorithms and applications. SIAM Journal on Computing, 16(1), 78–96.
  7. Chan, T. M. (1996). Optimal output-sensitive convex hull algorithms in two and three dimensions. Discrete & Computational Geometry, 16(4), 361–368.
  8. Genevay, A., Cuturi, M., Peyré, G., & Bach, F. (2016). Stochastic optimization for large-scale optimal transport. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 3440–3448.
  9. Graf, S., & Luschgy, H. (2000). Foundations of Quantization for Probability Distributions (Vol. 1730). Springer.
  10. Lloyd, S. (1982). Least Squares Quantization in PCM. IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 28(2), 129–137.
  11. Arthur, D., & Vassilvitskii, S. (2007). k-means++: The Advantages of Careful Seeding. Proceedings of the Eighteenth Annual ACM-SIAM Symposium on Discrete Algorithms, 1027–1035.
  12. Caglioti, E., Golse, F., & Iacobelli, M. (2015). A Gradient Flow Approach to Quantization of Measures. Mathematical Models and Methods in Applied Sciences, 25(10), 1845–1885. 10.1142/S0218202515500475
  13. Caglioti, E., Golse, F., & Iacobelli, M. (2018). Quantization of Measures and Gradient Flows: A Perturbative Approach in the 2-Dimensional Case. Annales de l’Institut Henri Poincaré C, Analyse Non Linéaire, 35(6), 1531–1555. 10.1016/j.anihpc.2017.12.003
  14. Iacobelli, M. (2019). Asymptotic Analysis for a Very Fast Diffusion Equation Arising from the 1D Quantization Problem. Discrete and Continuous Dynamical Systems, 39(9), 4929–4943. 10.3934/dcds.2019201
  15. Iacobelli, M., Patacchini, F., & Santambrogio, F. (2019). Weighted Ultrafast Diffusion Equations: From Well-Posedness to Long-Time Behaviour. Archive for Rational Mechanics and Analysis, 232(3), 1165–1206. 10.1007/s00205-018-01341-w
